interactions.summary: Jiang situates the Communist Manifesto in the 1848 revolutions and reads its opening specter language as the Geist of communism haunting old Europe.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang reading and interpreting Marx and Engels

interactions.summary: He reads the manifesto's indictment of the bourgeoisie and explains capitalism as an all-consuming force that destroys older relations to create economic man.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang reading and interpreting Marx and Engels

interactions.summary: He casts Marx as a Christian extension: Marx demands revolutionary participation, universal paradise, and a heaven without God, which cannot satisfy the need that produces Stalin and Mao.

speaker_notes.note: These segments include quoted manifesto language mixed with Jiang's interpretation.

claims.claim: Jiang reads the Communist Manifesto's specter as Marx's replacement Geist: communism is imagined as the inevitable world spirit that old authorities try to exorcise.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretation of the 1848 manifesto in the 2025-05-29 lecture.

claims.claim: The manifesto's power lies in diagnosing capitalism as a force that dissolves feudal, patriarchal, and family relations into naked economic self-interest.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's praise of the manifesto's diagnostic writing.

claims.claim: Marx extends Christianity by turning salvation into active revolutionary participation and universal worker paradise, but Jiang says utopia without God cannot satisfy religious desire.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's theological critique of Marxism in this lecture.

signature_moments.moment: The specter of communism becomes Jiang's evidence that Marx turns Geist into revolution.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It ties manifesto rhetoric back to the course's Hegelian frame.

signature_moments.moment: No one wants a five-star hotel heaven without God.
signature_moments.source_phrase: no one wants utopia without God
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It sharply explains why Jiang thinks secular paradise mutates into personality cults.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The person capitalism creates by reducing identity and relation to self-interest, money, and market position.
Marx's universal secular equivalent of salvation, which Jiang argues lacks God and therefore cannot fulfill religious longing.

chronology_notes.note: The Communist Manifesto is placed in 1848, after the potato famine and uprisings, as an international revolutionary document.
